content: A beginner's guide to setting up a malware analysis lab and understanding the fundamentals of static and dynamic analysis.
title: Getting Started with Malware Analysis

content: Introduction Malware analysis is the process of understanding the behavior, purpose, and impact of a suspicious file or program. Whether you’re responding to an incident or proactively hunting threats, having a solid foundation in malware analysis is essential for any security analyst. In this post, I’ll walk through how to get started — from setting up a safe lab environment to performing your first static and dynamic analysis.
title: Getting Started with Malware Analysis

content: Setting Up Your Lab Before touching any malicious file, you need an isolated environment. Never analyze malware on your host machine.
title: Getting Started with Malware Analysis

content: Recommended Tools Tool Purpose VMware / VirtualBox Virtualization platform FlareVM Windows-based malware analysis distro REMnux Linux-based reverse engineering distro Wireshark Network traffic capture Process Monitor Behavior monitoring
title: Getting Started with Malware Analysis

content: Basic Setup Steps Install a hypervisor (VMware Workstation or VirtualBox) Create a snapshot of a clean Windows VM before analysis Disable network or use an isolated virtual network Install FlareVM on the Windows VM Always revert to your clean snapshot after each analysis session.
title: Getting Started with Malware Analysis

content: Static Analysis Static analysis means examining a file without executing it. This is your first step — it’s safe and can reveal a lot.
title: Getting Started with Malware Analysis

content: Checking File Type Never trust file extensions. Use tools to identify the true file type: file suspicious.exe
title: Getting Started with Malware Analysis

content: Hashing Generate a hash to identify the sample and search threat intel platforms (VirusTotal, MalwareBazaar): sha256sum suspicious.exe md5sum suspicious.exe
title: Getting Started with Malware Analysis

content: Strings Extraction Extract readable strings from the binary — URLs, registry keys, and commands often appear here: strings suspicious.exe | grep -i "http" strings suspicious.exe | grep -i "cmd"
title: Getting Started with Malware Analysis

content: PE Header Analysis For Windows executables, inspect the PE structure using tools like PEview, PE-bear, or pestudio: Check imported functions (e.g., CreateProcess, VirtualAlloc, WriteProcessMemory — common in malware) Check section entropy — high entropy may indicate packed or encrypted code Check compilation timestamp (can be forged but still useful)
title: Getting Started with Malware Analysis

content: Dynamic Analysis Dynamic analysis means running the malware in a controlled environment and observing its behavior.
title: Getting Started with Malware Analysis

content: What to Monitor Filesystem changes — files created, modified, or deleted Registry changes — persistence mechanisms often live here Network activity — C2 communication, DNS queries Process activity — spawned child processes, injection attempts
title: Getting Started with Malware Analysis

content: Tools Workflow Run sample → Process Monitor → capture behavior → Wireshark → capture network traffic → Autoruns → check persistence → Process Hacker → inspect memory / injected threads
title: Getting Started with Malware Analysis

content: Common Malware Behaviors to Look For Persistence: HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run Process Injection: CreateRemoteThread, VirtualAllocEx C2 Beaconing: periodic HTTP/S or DNS requests to uncommon domains Lateral Movement: SMB traffic, credential dumping (LSASS access)
title: Getting Started with Malware Analysis

content: Reporting Your Findings A good analysis report should include: Executive Summary — what the malware does in plain language Indicators of Compromise (IOCs) — hashes, IPs, domains, file paths, registry keys MITRE ATT&CK Mapping — map observed behaviors to tactics and techniques Recommended Actions — containment, remediation steps
title: Getting Started with Malware Analysis

content: Conclusion Malware analysis is a skill built through practice. Start simple — analyze commodity malware like AgentTesla or Emotet samples from MalwareBazaar in your lab. Over time, you’ll develop the intuition to quickly triage and understand even complex threats. Stay curious, stay safe.
title: Getting Started with Malware Analysis
